title: Johnny Bedford

infobox.value: [Fort Worth, Texas](/wiki/Fort_Worth,_Texas "Fort Worth, Texas"), United States [Colleyville, Texas](/wiki/Colleyville,_Texas "Colleyville, Texas"), United States

(born January 6, 1983) is an American retired [mixed martial artist](/wiki/Mixed_martial_artist "Mixed martial artist") and [bare-knuckle boxer](/wiki/Bare-knuckle_boxer "Bare-knuckle boxer"). A professional from 2003 to 2017, he fought in the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship"), [Bellator](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA"), the [Legacy Fighting Alliance](/wiki/Legacy_Fighting_Alliance "Legacy Fighting Alliance"), and was a competitor on [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_TV "Spike TV")'s [The Ultimate Fighter: Team Bisping vs. Team Miller](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter:_Team_Bisping_vs._Team_Miller "The Ultimate Fighter: Team Bisping vs. Team Miller"). He also fought for the [Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") (BKFC), where he was a former [BKFC Bantamweight Champion](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#Bantamweight_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship").

**Johnny Bedford** (born January 6, 1983) is an American retired [mixed martial artist](/wiki/Mixed_martial_artist "Mixed martial artist") and [bare-knuckle boxer](/wiki/Bare-knuckle_boxer "Bare-knuckle boxer"). A professional from 2003 to 2017, he fought in the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship"), [Bellator](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA"), the [Legacy Fighting Alliance](/wiki/Legacy_Fighting_Alliance "Legacy Fighting Alliance"), and was a competitor on [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_TV "Spike TV")'s [The Ultimate Fighter: Team Bisping vs. Team Miller](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter:_Team_Bisping_vs._Team_Miller "The Ultimate Fighter: Team Bisping vs. Team Miller"). He also fought for the [Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") (BKFC), where he was a former [BKFC Bantamweight Champion](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#Bantamweight_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Bedford was born and raised in [Woodville, Ohio](/wiki/Woodville,_Ohio "Woodville, Ohio"). At the age of five, Bedford began wrestling year round. He and his family would travel all over the Midwest so Bedford could compete in various wrestling tournaments. He went on to wrestle for [Woodmore High School](/wiki/Woodmore_High_School "Woodmore High School"), where his father was the head wrestling coach. Bedford was a three-time state qualifier, placed in the state tournament twice, and during his senior year finished third in the state tournament with a record of 51–1.[[2]](#cite_note-Bio-2) After graduating from Woodmore he enrolled at [Cleveland State University](/wiki/Cleveland_State_University "Cleveland State University"). At Cleveland State, Bedford was on the wrestling team for a year and a half before he was removed from the school (as well as the wrestling squad) before later making the transition to a career in [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts").[[2]](#cite_note-Bio-2)

## Mixed martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts career")]

### Amateur career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Amateur career")]

Bedford began fighting in 2003 because he was a self-proclaimed "tough guy" that just wanted to prove how tough he was. Bedford proclaims his first six amateur fights took place in a barn where the promoter just asked the audience who wanted to fight and then would match them up by who was closest to one another's weight. With no commission in Ohio at the time, the fights were not sanctioned.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Early professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early professional career")]

After a successful amateur career, Bedford took a year and half off from fighting before being offered his first professional fight in 2006 for $200. With no income, Bedford took the fight on short notice and lost via submission in the first round. Bedford began fighting more frequently and by the end of 2009 Bedford held a 13–8–1 record.

### Bellator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bellator")]

Bedford was invited to fight for [Bellator](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA") in 2010.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The fight took place at [Bellator 19](/wiki/Bellator_19 "Bellator 19") in [Grand Prairie, Texas](/wiki/Grand_Prairie,_Texas "Grand Prairie, Texas"), on May 20, 2010. Bedford fought and defeated Jared Lopez via third-round TKO.

### Post-Bellator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-Bellator")]

At King of Kombat 9: Resurrection, Bedford fought future UFC fighter, [Edwin Figueroa](/wiki/Edwin_Figueroa "Edwin Figueroa"). Bedford lost the fight via TKO in the opening of the second round, handing Bedford his only knockout loss in his career. Four months later he bounced back with a first round submission over [WEC](/wiki/World_Extreme_Cagefighting "World Extreme Cagefighting") veteran [Frank Gomez](/wiki/Frank_Gomez "Frank Gomez").

### The Ultimate Fighter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The Ultimate Fighter")]

In 2011, Bedford had signed with the UFC to compete on [The Ultimate Fighter: Team Bisping vs. Team Miller](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter:_Team_Bisping_vs._Team_Miller "The Ultimate Fighter: Team Bisping vs. Team Miller") in Las Vegas. In the first episode, Bedford fought Carson Beebe to gain entry into the *Ultimate Fighter* house. Bedford defeated Beebe in the first round via submission (neck crank). He was selected as a part of Team Mayhem.

Bedford then fought Josh Ferguson in the third episode and won by unanimous decision after using his superior size to take down and control Ferguson.

In the semi-finals Bedford pleaded for the fight against Team Mayhem teammate, [John Dodson](/wiki/John_Dodson_(fighter) "John Dodson (fighter)"). The coaches and [Dana White](/wiki/Dana_White "Dana White") agreed with the fight, and matched the two up. After a closely contested first round, Bedford lost after being knocked unconscious one minute into round two. When asked if he knew where he was immediately after the fight, Bedford replied, "I'm in Ohio"; his home state.

### Ultimate Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ultimate Fighting Championship")]

Though he did not win the show, Bedford signed an exclusive contract with the UFC. He officially made his UFC debut on December 3, 2011, at [The Ultimate Fighter 14 Finale](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_14_Finale "The Ultimate Fighter 14 Finale") against *Ultimate Fighter* castmate [Louis Gaudinot](/wiki/Louis_Gaudinot "Louis Gaudinot"). Bedford dominated the entirety of the fight and won in the third round via TKO after hitting several knees to Gaudinot's body.

Bedford was expected to face [Eddie Wineland](/wiki/Eddie_Wineland "Eddie Wineland") on January 28, 2012, at [UFC on Fox 2](/wiki/UFC_on_Fox_2 "UFC on Fox 2"), replacing [Demetrious Johnson](/wiki/Demetrious_Johnson_(fighter) "Demetrious Johnson (fighter)") who had been pulled from the bout to be a participant in the UFC's inaugural [Flyweight](/wiki/Flyweight_(MMA) "Flyweight (MMA)") tournament.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, Wineland himself was forced out of the bout with an injury and replaced by promotional newcomer [Mitch Gagnon](/wiki/Mitch_Gagnon "Mitch Gagnon").[[7]](#cite_note-7) On January 25, 2012, the UFC announced the bout was cancelled due to Gagnon's alleged visa issues.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Bedford received his show-money of $8,000, despite the cancellation.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Bedford was expected to face [Nick Denis](/wiki/Nick_Denis "Nick Denis") at [UFC on Fox 3](/wiki/UFC_on_Fox_3 "UFC on Fox 3") on May 5, 2012.[[10]](#cite_note-10) However, Bedford was forced out of the bout with an injury and replaced by [Roland Delorme](/wiki/Roland_Delorme "Roland Delorme").[[11]](#cite_note-BedfordOut/DelormeIn-11)

After nearly a year off due to various injuries, Bedford returned on December 15, 2012, to face [Marcos Vinicius](/wiki/Marcos_Vinicius_(fighter) "Marcos Vinicius (fighter)") at [The Ultimate Fighter: Team Carwin vs. Team Nelson Finale](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter:_Team_Carwin_vs._Team_Nelson_Finale "The Ultimate Fighter: Team Carwin vs. Team Nelson Finale").[[12]](#cite_note-12) He won the fight via KO in the second round.

Bedford was expected to face [Erik Pérez](/wiki/Erik_P%C3%A9rez "Erik Pérez") on April 27, 2013, at [UFC 159](/wiki/UFC_159 "UFC 159").[[13]](#cite_note-13) However, Perez pulled out of the bout just days before the event citing an injury and was replaced by [Bryan Caraway](/wiki/Bryan_Caraway "Bryan Caraway").[[14]](#cite_note-14) He lost the back-and-forth fight via submission in the third round.

Bedford was expected to face [Hugo Viana](/wiki/Hugo_Viana_(fighter) "Hugo Viana (fighter)") on September 4, 2013, at [UFC Fight Night 28](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_28 "UFC Fight Night 28").[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, Bedford pulled out of the bout citing an injury and was replaced by [Wilson Reis](/wiki/Wilson_Reis "Wilson Reis").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Bedford faced [Rani Yahya](/wiki/Rani_Yahya "Rani Yahya") on April 11, 2014, at [UFC Fight Night 39](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_39 "UFC Fight Night 39").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The bout ended in a No Contest as an accidental clash of heads rendered Yahya unable to protect himself, forcing a referee stoppage at 0:39 of round 1.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Bedford argued vociferously that the accidental clash of heads should have been ruled a TKO victory in his favor.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

A rematch with Yahya was expected to take place on June 28, 2014, at [UFC Fight Night 44](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_44 "UFC Fight Night 44").[[20]](#cite_note-20) However, Yahya was forced out of the bout and replaced by Cody Gibson.[[21]](#cite_note-YahyaOut-21) For the second time in a row, his fight ended under somewhat controversial circumstances as Bedford was dropped by punches from Gibson and the fight was stopped by the referee just as Bedford appeared to be regaining consciousness, resulting in a first-round TKO loss.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The rescheduled rematch with [Rani Yahya](/wiki/Rani_Yahya "Rani Yahya") eventually took place on September 13, 2014, at [UFC Fight Night 51](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_51 "UFC Fight Night 51").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Yahya won via submission in the second round, and Bedford was subsequently released from the promotion shortly after.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Grappling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Grappling")]

On September 30, 2012, Johnny Bedford competed in a no gi super fight against Jimmy Flick (Flick Brother's MMA) at the American Grappling Federation's Dallas Fall Classic.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Johnny won the match by points.

## Bare knuckle boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bare knuckle boxing")]

Bedford faced [Nick Mamalis](/wiki/Nick_Mamalis "Nick Mamalis") at the inaugural [BKFC 1](/wiki/2018_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_1:_The_Beginning "2018 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") event held on June 2, 2018. He won the fight via TKO in the second round.

He made his sophomore appearance in the sport against Matt Murphy at [BKFC 3](/wiki/2018_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_3:_Adams_vs._Shewmaker "2018 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") on October 20, 2018. He won the fight via unanimous decision.

### BKFC Lightweight Tournament and championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: BKFC Lightweight Tournament and championship")]

Bedford entered the BKFC Lightweight tournament and faced Abdiel Velazquez at [BKFC 5](/wiki/2019_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_5:_Lobov_vs._Knight "2019 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") on April 6, 2019. He won the fight via fourth-round knockout, advancing to the tournament finals.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In the tournament final on June 22, 2019 at [BKFC 6](/wiki/2019_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_6:_Malignaggi_vs._Lobov "2019 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship"), Bedford defeated Reggie Barnett Jr. via unanimous decision to win BKFC and Police Gazette lightweight titles, the former later being renamed the BKFC Bantamweight Championship.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Bedford next faced [Charles Bennett](/wiki/Charles_Bennett_(fighter) "Charles Bennett (fighter)") at [BKFC 9](/wiki/2019_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_9:_Lobov_vs._Knight_II "2019 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") on November 16, 2019, in a non-title bout. He won the fight via second-round technical knockout.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Bedford was expected to make his first title defense against [Dat Nguyen](/wiki/Dat_Nguyen_(boxer) "Dat Nguyen (boxer)") at [BKFC 14](/wiki/2020_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship_14:_Palomino_vs._Alers "2020 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") on November 14, 2020. The fight however was canceled due to a Bedford injury. Bedford eventually lost his title belt to undefeated Dat Nguyen on February 5, 2021, in Lakeland, Florida.[[29]](#cite_note-bkfc14-29)

Bedford faced Reggie Barnett Jr. in a rematch for the vacant BKFC Bantamweight Championship at [BKFC 20](/wiki/2021_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_20:_Bedford_vs._Barnett "2021 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") on August 20, 2021. He won the fight via unanimous decision.

### Retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Retirement")]

On December 21, 2022, Beford announced his retirement from bare-knuckle fighting and other combat sports. He had been previously booked to fight Jarod Grant in March 2022 but withdrew from the fight.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Later, he was set to face Dat Nguyen in another title defense in January 2023. But the match was canceled after Bedford retired.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Championships and accomplishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Championships and accomplishments")]

### Mixed martial arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts")]

* **[Warrior Xtreme Cagefighting](/wiki/Warrior_Xtreme_Cagefighting "Warrior Xtreme Cagefighting")**
  + WXC Bantamweight Championship (One time)
* **[Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship")**
  + [BKFC Lightweight World Champion](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#Lightweight_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship")
  + Police Gazette Lightweight Bare Knuckle title

### Folkstyle wrestling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johnny_Bedford&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Folkstyle wrestling")]

* **[Ohio High School Athletic Association](/wiki/Ohio_High_School_Athletic_Association "Ohio High School Athletic Association")**
  + OHSAA Division 3 112 lb 6th place out of [Woodmore High School](/wiki/Woodmore_High_School "Woodmore High School") (2000)[[32]](#cite_note-Baum's_Page-32)
  + OHSAA Division 2 119 lb 3rd place out of [Woodmore High School](/wiki/Woodmore_High_School "Woodmore High School") (2001)[[32]](#cite_note-Baum's_Page-32)
